sentences.text: You apparently want to learn how it went on with my life during the German occupation.
In the beginning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were restricted, to be sure.

But even though there were horrifying scenes everywhere you looked, as I refresh my memory of those days-- That is to say, Jewish people, particularly the traditional ones, with beards, were caught in the streets and made to-- to clean the cobblestones on their knees or to shine the soldiers' boots, often spitting at them or making a mockery out of-- of these people by cutting their beards publicly.

It was all meant to dehumanize and demean them [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and to disgruntle their-- their-- their-- their whole [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being to make-- to make accepting subhumans out of them.
That was apparently going on all over the occupied territories.
But I eye-witnessed it already at the entry in Tarnow.
I can remember well when, in the Burek, the city's main square, they were catching Jews and humiliating them.
But even though there were brutalities, like spitting at them or hitting them or so, murder or killing did not occur.
In Tarnow and later in Gorlice.

The-- the beginning was humiliation and shrinking of-- of their world, pushing them into ever more compact living sections of cities, that turned eventually into closed ghettos, and then were liquidated.
All of it had some sort of a-- of a plan in it.
But we, at the time, were not aware of that.
We thought that these are outbursts that will pass by.
And actually, they did subside after the initial wave.
But no one dreamt of the kind of ruthless brutality and outright murder that followed.
That is best demonstrated when I tell you this story of my brother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother was, at the time, 19 years old.
He had rightly judged the coming persecution on hand of what he witnessed, as I described it, with the humiliations that he saw.
He then decided to escape into the eastern part of Poland, the part that was then occupied by the Soviet Union.

And we received letters, from my brother, that he is in contact with our family there, and that he has an opportunity, or may have an opportunity, to study medicine in Leningrad, which was his-- it-- it-- it was his dream to become a physician, since he was a youngster.
But my mother could not stop crying and could not stop worrying about him being with the Russians.
Some of this had to do with the traditional respect for religion.
And it-- it was just unthinkable that a godless society can be more humane than-- than the Western educated and talented Germans.
And so, she thought that he would be safer and better off if he returned to Gorlice, where our family was.
Eventually, he did return.
And of course, they were killed together, my mother and him, same day, same place.
And that leads to another story.

But-- but the moral of this story was that all too many of us believed that the Western Germans would still be more respectful of life than the ungodly Soviets, a fallacy that cost not only the life of my brother but of many who thought the same way.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I mentioned that my brother and my mother died together, they were taken away from Gorlice to Belzec.
But on the road, my mother was able to write a letter and throw it out of the train and instruct a Polish railroad worker to deliver it to us, who were already in a concentration camp in Plaszow.
And the Polish man, who who-- who found the letter, followed her instructions.
He came.
He found us.
And he delivered the letter.
We were, at the time, in Miller's camp.
It was 1942, end of '42, in Plaszow.
My father.
My father and I.
And in the letter, my mother said that my brother is sick with dysentery, and that they are headed for Belzec, and that we will likely never meet again.
And when we asked the Polish man, who delivered the letter, what really happened, he took out a bar of soap.
And he said, this is what they are.
They-- they gas them and take the body fat and produce soap.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Polish man, he was never paid for it.
And he did it, not only out of the kindness of his heart, but what he did actually carried a death sentence to him if he were caught.
These people took high risks.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You understand that the Polish, themselves, were considered subhumans.
And they had no rights of any kind.
If they followed the German dictate, they were permitted to stay alive.
But any violation against the German dictate was under death penalty.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that leads to another story.
I had an uncle.
His name was Janusz Keller.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was a very handsome and Aryan-looking Jew.
He was tall, wavy blond with blue eyes.
And he was chosen as a sort of middleman between the Jewish community and the Gestapo.
Amongst the Gestapo in Gorlice, there was a man by the name of Kuszka.
He was actually a chauffeur for the Gestapo, but, himself, an SS man a Gestapo officer.
And they had sort of a liaison relationship.
Now, the chief of Gestapo was a man by the name of Otto Friedrich.
Otto Friedrich, by a strange coincidence, looked very much like Janusz Keller.
He was also tall, handsome, wavy blond hair, and blue eyes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before the action to liquidate the ghetto of Gorlice, my uncle, Janusz Keller, he knew what's coming.
He gave us money.
And he told us, it is best if you escape the ghetto, at night, into the nearby forest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we did.
Remember that anything that violated the German law was under death penalty.
Of course, staying in the ghetto meant also death.
[SIGHS] Anyway, we-- it was summertime.
We escaped into the nearby forest.
And we had money on us, that he gave us.
When I say we, I mean my father, my mother, my brother, and myself.
We were all quite young people and vigorous people.
In the forest, there was a Polish man, a forester, who had a son.
And the son was a nice, young Polish man of above average intelligence.
He soon discovered us.
And he said, I cannot permit you entry into my house or anywhere near my family.
But as long as you remain here, I will provide you with food and with information.
And so he did.
We tried to pay him off.
He did not ask for money or accept any.
And he was totally truthful and, at least for the brief period that we were in that forest, he was-- he was a life saver.
I don't remember his name.
And I-- I regret it to this day.
I cannot tell his name, no.
But this was in the forest next to Gorlice.
When the action-- he described to us a whole action, what is going on in town, with the Jews.
It was frightening.
I may talk about this later.
But he provided us with food, bread and butter and buttermilk and things that-- that peasants have handy, and this vital information.
And eventually, when-- when the-- the murderous action of the Germans was over and everything calmed down in the city, he-- he communicated with those that remained in the city that we trusted.
And the decision was made that we should return.
It is now safe.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we spent, in that forest, about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six days.
And if it was not for this Polish man or if he was evil, we could have easily lost our life right there.
By the way, while the history of the Polish is antisemitic-- that is well-established-- it was actually rooted by the teachings of the Catholic Church.
That was the main ingredient in feeding the antisemitism in Eastern Poland.

But by a-- by a-- by a strange twist, of-- of-- made by Germans, themselves, they became, in a certain way, allied, since the brutality of the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was directed not only at the Jews but at the Poles, as well.
And now, suddenly, the Jews and the Poles had a mutual enemy.
It is apparently for that reason that I was repeatedly helped by Polish people.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] While before the war, they were not all that kind, as the war went on and as more and more Polish suffered the-- the indignities and the rest of-- of the German murderers, they developed a certain alliance with the Jews, at that time, at that period of time.
This goes back to this man in the forest who aided us.
But I will go back to the story that I eye-witnessed about about Otto Friedrich, the chief of the Gestapo in Gorlice, because I already mentioned his name.
And I mentioned the name of my uncle, Janusz Keller.
I was babysitting his child-- by the way, he and his whole family were murdered.
But-- but at the time, I believe it was 19-- it was early 1942.
It could have been late in '41.
But the windows of my uncle's apartment looked down at a Polish retention tank of the Polish police.
They had a retention tank there that they used for Polish people who got drunk.
They-- they put them in there until they sobered up.
And I was babysitting my cousin, my little cousin.
It got late.
And we were not permitted to be in the street after dark, so I stayed overnight there.
And I slept by the window that looked into that backyard of the Polish police, with that retention tank.
I was awakened, after midnight, by bright light and the sound of a motorcycle.
When I looked down, I saw Otto Friedrich, the chief of the Gestapo.
He was in his shiny boots and white gloves on a motorcycle.
Apparently, the Polish police had found eight Gypsy children in a-- in a forest nearby.
It could've been the same forest-- probably was-- where we were hiding.
And they brought them into this retention tank.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Otto Friedrich was apparently notified by the Polish police of the find, that they had a group of gypsy children in the retention tank.
He-- he-- he ordered the Polish policemen to open the tank.
And the children were attracted by the motorcycle and the light, and so they start-- they came running at-- at Otto Friedrich.
He-- he calmly took out his revolver.
It was a shiny one.
He shot one after another in the head.
And with his boots, he made a heap, a neat heap, of all the eight children.
He killed one after another.
They-- the-- the big ones came first, so they were killed first.
The little ones didn't even understand.
They thought it's firecrackers.
When it was all over, he called the Polish policeman, made him shine his boots, the ones that he used to make a neat heap out of the children, and to clean the backyard.
He drove off on the motorcycle as if nothing happened.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was a story of Otto Friedrich.
[SIGHS] This is one of the stories that I witnessed, simply because I was by the window looking down at the courtyard.
[SIGHS] Until the outbreak of the war between Germany and the Soviet Union, I don't remember having seen any overt killing or murder in the streets.
I have seen demeaning brutality, disrespect, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] every kind of chicanery you can think of, but not killing.
The killing started with the moment the Germans apparently got rid of Soviet intervention.
Once the Soviet Union was attacked-- I'm talking about June 22, 1941-- the killing started [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to show itself, obviously, openly, in the streets.
The first killing I witnessed is when I was sick with pneumonia.
In those days, this was late in 1941, I came down with pneumonia.
And in those days, pneumonia was a deathly disease.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had just gotten a little better.
My family was still intact.
It was a Saturday afternoon, beautiful day.
I remember it like today.
It was a-- it was-- it might have been in-- in September or so of 1941.
A group of Germans, apparently the sonderkommando, came into town and started shooting Jewish people, at random, in the street.
As I looked out the window, Jews were running.
And Germans were having great fun target shooting.
I believe we had eight casualties that day.
Most of them were senile or-- or mentally impaired people.
One of them was an 18-year-old child that I remember.
Pardon me, I'm wrong.
An eight-year-old child, that I remember.
The others were older and many of them mentally impaired people.
I remember this as the first time that I saw open murder in the street.
This brings me to your question.
My reaction was of horror, of course.

But even though we witnessed these kind of horrifying incidents-- by now, outright, open murder-- still, when we heard from others the horrifying descriptions of what went on in-- in ghettos that were liquidated, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we simply lacked the-- the-- the fantasy to perceive what that means, to-- to understand this.
We couldn't believe that such a thing is-- is conceivable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of the things that-- the German brutality and subhuman behavior was of such magnitude that it didn't-- it didn't square well with the normal human mind.

When I returned from the forest, I returned-- I-- I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I was in the forest, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans had sent in a sonderkommando, with their faithful allies the Ukrainians.
The Ukrainians were murder assistants that were highly proficient.
They simply were outstanding killers.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The description I got then and, again, when I visited the same place, the-- the-- the little-- the little marketplace in Gorlice, again, two years ago, I spoke to the Polish who lived around it.
It was the same description I got.
Those people, who were aged, sick, diseased, crippled, they were simply grabbed by arms and legs and thrown on hay wagons, one on top of another, and beaten with rifle butts.
The blood was draining all over.
Those on the bottom were suffocating.
They were simply dragged out by horse-- by horse-drawn hay wagons to the-- to where the Jewish cemetery was and buried alive.
According to-- now this is not eyewitness.
This is according to the description of the Polish people who lived around [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the place of-- where the Jews were rounded up in Gorlice.
And also, the lady, the Polish lady, who verified it, who is-- who lives within the Jewish cemetery, today, and did at that time, too.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This-- this story, had been-- we had-- we were told similar stories occurring in other places.
We could not believe it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But, apparently, the Germans had developed a pattern on how to go about it.
And the same thing came to our ghetto.
When I returned, they had already done away with all the Jews that they thought are useless.
These are the elders, the sick, the crippled, and the children.
Those who were fit, who were-- who-- young people, vigorous people, and so, were again, they were-- they were displaced into an ever smaller living quarter for use, as slave labor, as it turned out.
We came into the city at the time.
And we became part of this residual group that was meant to be shipped for slave labor, as it turned out, to the camp in Plaszow.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
